project_overview.project_name: Road Sector Climate Resilience Program SOP-1 (RSCRP SOP-1)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To enhance climate risk management in Bolivia's road sector, and improve climate resilience and road safety of specific road sections
project_overview.beneficiaries: 165,000 direct and indirect beneficiaries (approximately 83,000 women) in municipalities of Chimore, Ivirgarzama, Guanay, and Monteagudo; approximately 9,000 vehicles daily use the existing roads

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with regional focus on three specific road sections
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Bolivia faces agitated and conflictive social and political context with recurrent strikes and mobilizations; however, not classified as fragile state. Project areas are vulnerable to climate-related disasters (floods and landslides)

key_components.description: Road sector climate resilience diagnosis; development of institutional and infrastructure management strategy for climate-resilient road sector; capacity building and training for ABC officials on climate risk, road safety, gender considerations, and contract management; road safety design improvements; integrated management information system; gender action plan; and feasibility studies for future interventions

key_components.component_name: Bridge and Road Upgrading for Climate Resilience and Road Safety
key_components.description: Three main interventions: (2a) Chimore-Ivirgarzama - construction of bridge, replacement of culverts, footbridges, and overpass accesses (US$32.0M); (2b) Guanay Bridge - replacement of existing bridge and construction of accesses (US$15.1M); (2c) Molino-Monteagudo - rehabilitation of existing road and construction of bypass to avoid urban area (US$43.9M); (2d) Gender and social inclusion investments (US$1.0M)

Establishment and operation of Project Coordination and Support Team (PCST), technical advisory consultancy, monitoring and evaluation, environmental and social commitment plan implementation, financial audits, training, and project management oversight

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Administradora Boliviana de Carreteras (ABC) - Bolivian Road Agency, a decentralized entity of the Ministry of Public Works, Services and Housing (MOPSV). Ministry of Development Planning (MPD) is the signatory of the loan agreement
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination and Support Team (PCST) headed by full-time Project Coordinator; Technical Advisory (TA) consultancy with 3-5 long-term consultants and drawdown mechanism for short-term support; coordination between ABC HQ (La Paz) and regional offices in La Paz, Cochabamba, and Chuquisaca departments; collaboration with Vice Ministry of Public Investment and External Financing (VIPFE)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (September 2023); open international competitive bidding for larger contracts; open national competitive bidding for smaller contracts; quality-cost-based selection or consultant qualification-based selection for consulting services; use of STEP system for procurement plan tracking
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results framework with PDO and intermediate indicators; semiannual reports on results indicators; annual reports on fund use; semiannual financial reports; mid-term review at approximately 30 months; dedicated M&E team within PCST; annual supervision missions by World Bank; monitoring system through ABC; grievance redress mechanism with online registry

identified_risks.risk_description: Challenging short- to medium-term macroeconomic outlook due to potential El Niño impacts, large fiscal deficits, constrained domestic and international financing, pressure on international reserves, potential inflation increases, and imported goods price increases
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Adequate contingencies in project cost estimates; financing for land acquisition and compensation included as eligible project financing; close monitoring of macroeconomic conditions

identified_risks.risk_description: Low capacity of ABC in procurement and financial management for IPF; high staff turnover causing delays in procurement and financial reporting; lack of centralized information system; dependency on MEFP for timely payment processing; low capacity in accounts payable management; delays in contracting financial audits
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Hiring fiduciary staff with WB procurement and FM knowledge; training of fiduciary staff; contracting independent Technical Advisory consultancy with qualified fiduciary staff; preparation of comprehensive Project Operations Manual; enhancement of in-house FM system (SICOPP) with interfaces to other systems; close monitoring of fund availability; appointment of independent auditors no later than 6 months after effectiveness with minimum 3-year contract

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential changes in political commitment; agitated and conflictive social and political context with recurrent strikes and mobilizations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong political ownership and continued commitment during project life; improved cross-institutional coordination; maintenance of designed implementation arrangements

identified_risks.risk_description: Need to strengthen road sector strategy to address climate change and road safety comprehensively; limited utilization of existing road asset management tools
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of institutional and infrastructure management strategy for climate-resilient road sector; capacity building for ABC; implementation of hydroclimatic model results manual

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of designing climate-resilient infrastructure in challenging topography; need for updated designs incorporating climate considerations; technical capacity constraints at ABC
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Updated designs with increased lookback and verification periods for bridges (200-year design, 300-year verification) and culverts (50-year design); road safety audits at multiple stages; technical advisory support; capacity building for ABC staff

identified_risks.risk_description: ABC's limited capacity to manage complex projects; high staff turnover; weak coordination between ABC units and with stakeholders; challenges in environmental and social risk management
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of PCST with dedicated Project Coordinator; Technical Advisory consultancy support; Project Operations Manual with detailed procedures; capacity building and training programs; enhanced communication channels; lessons learned from previous projects incorporated into design

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risks including: contamination of rivers from spills/discharges; alteration of hydrological cycles; nuisance to communities (noise, dust, vibration); erosion acceleration; inadequate traffic management; waste and hazardous materials handling; vegetation loss; environmental liabilities; borrow pit and water pond management; occupational health and safety issues; economic and property displacement; labor influx; weak labor management; community tensions; debts with workers; road safety risks; social exclusion of vulnerable groups; conflictive social context; substantial SEA/SH risk due to labor influx, poverty, sexism culture, high statutory rape incidence, and high feminicide rates
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Spill prevention and response plans; erosion control measures; noise and dust reduction strategies; proper waste management; vegetation preservation; strengthened institutional capacity; enhanced road safety measures; occupational health and safety protocols; water harvesting pond and borrow pit management; consultation with local communities and vulnerable groups; labor influx management; timely payments and adequate working conditions; social inclusion promotion; gender-based violence prevention and response programs; grievance redress mechanism with designated channels for GBV/SEA/SH complaints; linkage with SLIMs and DNAs; training of community promoters; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP); Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); Gender Action Plan (GAP); Labor Management Plan; environmental and social management plans; resettlement plans

identified_risks.risk_description: Recent increase in community and civil actions involving road network that can delay implementation; need for effective community participation and grievance resolution; underrepresentation of women in decision-making; potential social exclusion of vulnerable groups
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Detailed social assessments for each infrastructure investment; meaningful consultations with affected communities; effective grievance redress mechanisms operating throughout implementation; culturally appropriate mechanisms; launch workshops before commencement of works; opportunity for communities to identify small investments with high social impact; gender-sensitive consultations; participation of women, elderly, youth, and people with reduced mobility in design consultations; at least 50% of complementary investments allocated to women's priorities

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level grievance mechanism to be established by ABC within 30 days after effectiveness; online GRM system for registry and follow-up of complaints with details on receipt, processing, response, and status; multiple channels including email, mayor's office, contractor mailboxes, in-person complaints, and World Bank GRS referrals; designated channels staffed by trained professionals for GBV/SEA/SH complaints; linkage with SLIMs and DNAs for GBV service provision; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism available for project-affected communities
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender Action Plan (GAP) to be developed addressing: gender-sensitive infrastructure designs; gender-inclusive consultations and communications; prevention and response to gender-based violence (GBV); sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA) and sexual harassment (SH) risk mitigation; linkage with SLIMs and DNAs; training of community promoters on healthy conflict resolution, healthy relationships, and healthy parenting; women economic empowerment in road conservation microenterprises; capacity building for women in ABC (at least one social specialist with gender experience at HQ and one in each of three regional offices); gender-responsive data collection and analysis; gender-sensitive budgeting; at least 50% of complementary investments (Intervention 2d) allocated to women's priorities; pedestrian sidewalks and non-motorized transport features to improve women's mobility and safety; improved lighting and visibility for women's sense of security; accessibility features for elderly and people with reduced mobility; target of 82,500 women beneficiaries out of 165,000 total beneficiaries
vulnerable_groups: Women (particularly in rural areas facing time poverty and mobility constraints)
People with disabilities and reduced mobility
Rural populations with limited access to services
Children (road safety risks in school areas)
Potential survivors of gender-based violence and sexual exploitation/abuse

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project incorporates conflict-sensitive approaches through: meaningful community consultations and engagement mechanisms; grievance redress mechanisms designed to address community tensions; social assessments for each intervention; launch workshops to explain project and responsibilities; active and continuous involvement of affected communities; mechanisms to address underlying conflicts with local communities/organizations; communication strategies for environmental and social aspects; linkage with local authorities and community organizations; recognition of agitated and conflictive social and political context with recurrent strikes and mobilizations; measures to ensure timely payments to avoid debts with workers and local providers that could fuel tensions
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Bolivia faces significant fragility factors: (1) High vulnerability to climate-related disasters - ranked 10th globally in climate risk index with 84 extreme weather events between 1965-2020 causing US$3.7 billion in damages (9% of 2019 GDP) and affecting 9 million people; (2) Geographic and topographical challenges - landlocked, sparsely populated, with steep Andean mountains prone to landslides and eastern floodplains vulnerable to floods; (3) Economic vulnerabilities - constrained fiscal space, declining gas exports, weak private investment, large fiscal deficits, pressure on international reserves; (4) Social vulnerabilities - high poverty (15.2% internationally, 32% in rural areas, 22% among indigenous peoples), gender inequalities, high violence against women (one of highest feminicide rates in LAC), limited rural accessibility (80% of rural population lacks access to all-weather roads); (5) Institutional weaknesses - ABC capacity constraints, high staff turnover, weak coordination across government levels; (6) Political instability - agitated and conflictive social and political context with recurrent strikes and mobilizations
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project addresses security through: (1) Road safety as core PDO outcome with target to reduce traffic fatalities from 5 to 3 annually in intervened areas (40% reduction); (2) Road safety audits at multiple design and construction stages with focus on speed management; (3) Enhanced road safety features including marked paved shoulders, improved markings and signs, safety barriers, speed management/traffic calming, improved signalization and pedestrian crossings; (4) Pedestrian sidewalks and non-motorized transport features for vulnerable users; (5) Improved lighting and visibility for women's sense of security; (6) Occupational health and safety protocols during construction; (7) Labor management plans to address labor influx risks; (8) SEA/SH prevention and response programs with designated GRM channels; (9) Community engagement and communication strategies; (10) Road designs accounting for climate hazards (flooding, landslides) that could cause accidents
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Involuntary resettlement and economic displacement identified as significant social risk. Mitigation measures include: (1) ESS 5 (Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement) triggered; (2) Resettlement Plans to be prepared for each intervention; (3) Land and resettlement expenditures (US$8.8 million) eligible for project financing; (4) Detailed social assessments for each infrastructure investment; (5) Consultations with affected populations; (6) Protocols and mechanisms to ensure participation of different population segments in consultations; (7) Timely payments and compensation to affected persons; (8) Grievance redress mechanisms for resettlement-related complaints; (9) Capacity building for ABC in resettlement management; (10) Lessons learned from previous projects incorporated into design
